sections.heading: Overview
sections.body:
MongoDB Search allows you to sort the results in ascending or descending order on
fields that you define in your MongoDB Search index. You can sort by the
following field types using the `sort` option:

- `boolean`

- `date`

- `number` (integer, float, and double values)

- `objectId`

- `uuid`

- `string` (indexed as the [token](bson-data-types-token) type)

You can also sort by the score of the documents in the results and by
[Sort by Null and Missing Values](fts-sort-null).

sections.heading: sort Option Compatibility
sections.body:
Atlas supports both non-sharded and sharded sort queries on
all major and minor releases for MongoDB 7.0 and later versions.

sections.heading: sort Option Limitations
sections.body:
- You can't sort on fields of [embeddedDocuments](bson-data-types-embedded-documents) type.

- You can't use the `sort` option with the deprecated
  [knnBeta](knn-beta-ref)
  operator.

sections.heading: Usage
sections.body:
To sort your MongoDB Search results, you must do the following:

- Create a MongoDB Search index on the fields to sort the results by.

  To sort on `boolean`, `date`, `number`, `UUID`, and
  `objectId` fields, use dynamic or static mappings. To sort on
  string fields, you must use [dynamic mappings](static-dynamic-mappings) to index the field as the `token` type.

- Create and run your query with the `sort` option against the fields
  you defined in the index for sorting. To learn more, see
  [sort syntax](sort-syntax-ref).

sections.heading: Sort by Boolean, Date, Numeric, UUID, ObjectId, or String Field
sections.body:
The `sort` option takes a document that specifies the fields to sort
by and the respective sort order. MongoDB Search follows the [MongoDB
comparison order](manual/reference/bson-type-comparison-order) for the
supported data types. It treats UUID values like `BinData`. To learn
more, see [non-existent fields](manual/reference/bson-type-comparison-order).

You can specify the following sort order to sort your results by:

| 1  | Sort in ascending order.When you sort in ascending order, MongoDB Search returns documents with missing values before documents with values. |
| -- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| -1 | Sort in descending order.                                                                                                                    |

sections.heading: Sort by Score And a Unique Field
sections.body:
You can also sort by score in ascending or descending order. The
`sort` option takes a document that specifies the [$meta](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/meta) expression, which requires the
`searchScore` value.

> **Example:** Suppose your application allows users to skip to the last page of the
> search results. The following example sorts the results by score in
> ascending order so that the document with the lowest score displays at
> the top of the results:
>
> ```json
> sort: {score: {$meta: "searchScore", order: 1}}
> ```

You can use `sort` to also ensure that the results have a determined
order when multiple documents in the results have identical scores. For
example, if you sort the results by a unique field, such as a date field
named `lastUpdated` as shown in the following example, MongoDB Search returns
results with identical scores in a determined order:

> **Example:** 
>
> ```
> sort: {score: {$meta: "searchScore"}, lastUpdated: 1}
> ```

However, if you don't specify a unique field to sort the results by,
MongoDB Search returns the results sorted by score in descending order. MongoDB Search
returns results with identical scores or values in an arbitrary order.
The following example doesn't sort the results by a unique field.

> **Example:** 
>
> ```shell
> sort: {score: {$meta: "searchScore"}}
> ```

To learn more, see [relevance scoring](scoring-ref).

codeBlocks.content: sort: {score: {$meta: "searchScore", order: 1}}
sort: {score: {$meta: "searchScore"}, lastUpdated: 1}
sort: {score: {$meta: "searchScore"}}

sections.heading: Sort Arrays
sections.body:
MongoDB Search flattens the arrays for sorting.

> **Example:** Consider the following array:
>
> ```shell
> [4, [1, [8,5], 9], 2]
> ```
>
> MongoDB Search flattens the preceding array similar to the following:
>
> ```shell
> 4, 1, 8, 5, 9, 2
> ```
>
> For an ascending sort, MongoDB Search uses `1` to compare the array to other
> values. For a descending sort, MongoDB Search uses `9` to compare the array
> to other values.

When comparing with elements inside an array:

- For an ascending sort, MongoDB Search compares the smallest elements of the
  array or performs a less than (`<`) comparison.

  > **Example:** MongoDB Search sorts results in the following order if you sort by numbers
  > in ascending order:
  >
  > ```shell
  > -20
  > [-3, 12] // <- -3 comes before 5.
  > 5
  > [6, 18]  // <- 6 comes after 5.
  > 13
  > 14
  > ```

- For a descending sort, MongoDB Search compares the largest elements of the
  array or performs a greater than (`>`) comparison.

  > **Example:** MongoDB Search sorts results in the following order if you sort by numbers
  > in descending order:
  >
  > ```shell
  > [6, 18]  // <- 18 comes before 14.
  > 14
  > 13
  > [-3, 12] // <- 12 comes after 13.
  > 5
  > -20
  > ```

sections.heading: Sort Arrays with Multiple Types
sections.body:
When sorting over array fields that contain values of multiple
BSON (Binary Javascript Object Notation) types, MongoDB Search selects a representative element from the array
to use for comparison according to the [MongoDB comparison and sort order](manual/reference/bson-type-comparison-order) by default.

- For an ascending sort, MongoDB Search uses the element with the
  lowest BSON (Binary Javascript Object Notation) type.

- For a descending sort, MongoDB Search uses the element with the
  highest BSON (Binary Javascript Object Notation) type.

If there are multiple values of the same BSON (Binary Javascript Object Notation) type
in the array, standard sort behavior for the selected
type applies.

> **Example:** Consider the following array:
>
> ```shell
> [ 'foo', null, 15, true, false ]
> ```
>
> - For an ascending sort, MongoDB Search uses `null`, as it
>   is the lowest [supported](bson-data-types) BSON (Binary Javascript Object Notation) type.
>
> - For a descending sort, MongoDB Search uses `true`, as it
>   is the highest BSON (Binary Javascript Object Notation) type in the array and MongoDB Search ranks `true`
>   values above `false` values.
>
> However, if you set `noData: highest` in your
> `sort` [sort syntax](sort-syntax-ref), MongoDB Search considers
> null and missing values as the highest BSON (Binary Javascript Object Notation) type.
> For the example array, the following behavior applies:
>
> - For an ascending sort, MongoDB Search uses `15`, as
>   it is now the lowest BSON (Binary Javascript Object Notation) type in the array.
>
> - For a descending sort, MongoDB Search uses `null`, as it
>   is now the highest BSON (Binary Javascript Object Notation) type in the array.
>
> To learn more, see [Sort by Null and Missing Values](fts-sort-null).

For an example, see [Sort on Multi-Typed Arrays](fts-sort-eg-null-multiple-types).

codeBlocks.content: [ 'foo', null, 15, true, false ]

sections.heading: Sort by Null and Missing Values
sections.body:
MongoDB Search treats null values as equal to missing and empty values, and the
order of documents with these values is non-deterministic when sorting.

By default, MongoDB Search follows the [MongoDB comparison and sort order](manual/reference/bson-type-comparison-order) and considers null values
as lower than all other [supported](bson-data-types) BSON (Binary Javascript Object Notation) types.
Therefore, null values appear at the top of the results during
an ascending sort and at the bottom during a descending sort.

To configure where null values appear in the results, specify the `noData`
field in your `sort` [sort syntax](sort-syntax-ref). The `noData`
field takes the following values:

- `lowest` (default): Sets null values as the lowest BSON (Binary Javascript Object Notation) type during sorting.
  Sorts null values at the top of the results during an
  ascending sort and at the bottom during a descending sort.

- `highest`: Sets null values as the highest BSON (Binary Javascript Object Notation) type during sorting.
  Sorts null values at the bottom of the results during an
  ascending sort and at the top during a descending sort.

> **Note:** The same behavior applies when sorting on
> [Sort Arrays with Multiple Types](fts-sort-arrays-multiple-types)
> that contain null or missing values.

For examples, see [Sort by Null Values](fts-sort-eg-null) and
[Sort on Multi-Typed Arrays](fts-sort-eg-null-multiple-types).

sections.heading: Sort on Embedded Documents Array Fields
sections.body:
To sort the parent documents by an embedded document field, you must do the
following:

- Index the parents of the embedded document child field as the
  [document](bson-data-types-document) type.

- Index the child field with string values within the embedded document
  as the [token](bson-data-types-token) type. For child fields
  with number and date values, enable [dynamic mappings](static-dynamic-mappings) to index those fields automatically.

MongoDB Search sorts on parent documents only. It doesn't sort the child fields
within an array of documents. For an example, see [Sort Example](embedded-document-query-examples).

sections.heading: Consistency
sections.body:
MongoDB Search indexes are eventually [consistent](index-eventual-consistency-and-latency), and values returned in
results might be different from values used in sorting.

sections.heading: Performance
sections.body:
This feature optimizes queries that use [$search](pipe.$search) with
[$limit](manual/reference/operator/aggregation/limit) as a subsequent stage. If MongoDB Search needs to sort all
documents in the collection, the response might be slow.

sections.heading: Scoring
sections.body:
MongoDB Search returns [relevance scoring](scoring-ref) for all documents in the
results. However, you might see higher scoring documents after lower
scoring documents because the order of documents in the results is based
on the sort criteria unless you explicitly sort by score.

sections.heading: Syntax
Examples
sections.body:

`sample_airbnb.listingsAndReview`, or a custom collection named
`users`.***➤ Use the **Select your language** drop-down menu to set the
language of the examples in this section.***drivers

package com.mongodb.drivers;

javac SortByDate.java
java SortByDate
kotlin SortByDate.kt
node sort-by-date.js

import datetime
import pymongo

# connect to your Atlas cluster
client = pymongo.MongoClient('<connection-string>')

# define pipeline
pipeline = [
  {'$search': {
      'index': 'default',
      'compound': {
        'filter': {'wildcard': {'query': 'Summer*', 'path': 'title'}},
        'must': {'near': {
          "path": "released", 
          "origin": datetime.datetime(2014, 4, 18, 0, 0, 0, 0), 
          "pivot": 13149000000
      }}},
      'sort': { 'released': -1 }}},
  {'$limit': 5},
  {'$project': {'_id': 0, 'title': 1, 'released': 1, 'score': {'$meta': 'searchScore'}}}
]

# run pipeline
result = client['sample_mflix']['movies'].aggregate(pipeline)

# print results
for i in result:
    print(i)
python sort-by-date.py
